content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 21, 2017

Bruce Gibson
Monterey County Board of Supervisors
1055 Monterey, Room D430
San Luis Obispo, CA 93408-1003

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-188

Dear Mr. Gibson:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) | FPPC Ops. 71.) Additionally, the Commission does not provide advice relating to past conduct. This letter should not be construed as assistance on any conduct that may have already taken place. (See Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).)

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

QUESTION

Upon the dissolution of Cayucos Fire Protection District, may you take part in county discussion and decisions regarding the extension of the San Luis Obispo County Fire into the area formerly served by the district despite owning a home in which you reside in Cayucos?

CONCLUSION

Based upon the facts provided, you have established that you may take part in decisions regarding the extension of the San Luis Obispo County Fire into Cayucos under the public generally exception for decisions with limited neighborhood effects.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-188
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are the San Luis Obispo, District Two, County Supervisor and own a home, in which
you reside, in Cayucos within District Two. Fire protection services in Cayucos are currently
provided by the Cayucos Fire Protection District (the "CFPD"), an independently governed special
district established in 1948. The CFPD currently has primary fire service responsibility within the
urban area of Cayucos (population 2,592).

On July 6, 2017, the CFPD governing board voted to apply to the San Luis Obispo County
Local Agency Formation Commission ("LAFCO") for dissolution. The CFPD will continue to
provide fire services until LAFCO acts on that application. You are not a voting or alternate
member of LAFCO. If the CFPD dissolution is approved, San Luis Obispo County would be the
successor agency (to receive CFPD assets) and may agree to provide fire service in the urban area
of Cayucos. Since the county is not mandated by state statute to provide this service, any decision to
provide fire service in Cayucos might require action by the San Luis Obispo County Board of
Supervisors.

The county currently provides fire service in other parts of its unincorporated areas as "San
Luis Obispo County Fire" through a contract with the state fire service CalFIRE. The funding
provided to CalFIRE by the county supports fire service to a population of approximately 132,500.
The total population of the county is approximately 275,000.

If San Luis Obispo County Fire extends service to the former CFPD area, no change to
property tax or fire tax rates is currently contemplated. If any such tax change is proposed (for
Cayucos or any other area of the county), it would require separate county action and any potential
conflicts of interest would be analyzed separately at that time.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
A conflict of interest may arise only when it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on the official or his or her immediate family "distinguishable from its
effect on the public generally." (Section 87103.)

For purposes of this advice, the only interest you have identified is your interest in your
residence in Cayucos. Generally, an official is disqualified from any decision that has a foreseeable
and material effect on an interest in real property. However, under the facts provided it is
unnecessary to determine if there is a foreseeable and material effect on your property. Even
assuming a foreseeable and material effect, an official is not disqualified from a decision if the
effect of the decision on the official's interest is indistinguishable from the effect on the public
generally. This is commonly referred to as the public generally rule.

Regulation 18703(e)(3), which provides a public generally rule for decisions with "limited
neighborhood effects," provides that the effect on an official's economic interest is
indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally, if the official can establish:

File No. A-17-188
Page No. 3

"The decision affects residential real property limited to a specific location, and the decision establishes, amends, or eliminates ordinances that restrict on-street parking, impose traffic controls, deter vagrancy, reduce nuisance or improve public safety, provided the body making the decision gathers sufficient evidence to support the need for the action at the specific location."

As adopted, this exception for decisions regarding public nuisances and public safety, permits officials to take part in decisions as a matter of public policy where there is sufficient evidence supporting the public purpose for the action, and the action widely applies to a specific location with no unique effect on the official

In this case, a decision to extend fire protection to the properties within Cayucos and its 2,592 residents is unquestionably a decision to improve public safety that widely applies to the specific location, and there is no indication of a unique effect on your residence. Considering that fire protection is a basic matter of public safety and that the city's decisions are necessitated by the dissolution of the Cayucos Fire Protection District, we also find sufficient evidence supporting the need to make the decisions. Accordingly, the facts provided establish that you may take part in decisions, under the public generally rule, to extend San Luis Obispo County Fire into Cayucos.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Jack C. Woodside
General Counsel

By: Brian G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division

BGL:jgl

discussion and decisions regarding the extension of the San Luis Obispo County Fire into the area 
formerly served by the district despite owning a home in which you reside in Cayucos?

regarding the extension of the San Luis Obispo County Fire into Cayucos under the public generally 
exception for decisions with limited neighborhood effects.

CONCLUSION: Based upon the facts provided, you have established that you may take part in decisions
